The School-Treat

Gerda had refused point-blank to invite Darnley and Christie to supper on Monday night, thus bringing to nothing ChristieтАЩs premonition in the stubble-field. And now it was the middle of the long, sunlit afternoonтБатАФrelaxed, autumnal, mellowтБатАФof Mr.┬аValleyтАЩs great gala-day.

The f├кte was held in the vicarage-glebe, adjoining that portion of the churchyard-wall behind which rose the now four-months-old tombstone of the youthful Mr.┬аRedfern.

The young men and boys of the village, encouraged at their game by Mr.┬аUrquhart and Darnley Otter, were engaged in an interminable cricket-match, a match played between those who lived west of the church and those who lived east.

When Wolf first left his employerтАЩs library, which he did some half-hour later than the squire himself, and entered the school-treat field, he felt nervous and irritable. Everyone he knew in the world seemed to be gathered in that enclosure; and as he stealthily shuffled along the edge of the churchyard, he felt as if he would like to hide himself from them all, down in the silent earth along with young Redfern!

He found himself at a spot where the wall was very low, and, turning his back upon the crowded scene, leaned there for a while unnoticed, gazing at the great perpendicular tower. With the shouts and laughter in his ears, that tower looked incredibly massive and silent. What ebbing and flowing of human lives had it not seen, since unknown hands in the reign of the first Tudor piled it up there, stone upon stone!

Well, at least it was something to face the disquietudes of his own life in the presence of masonry like this, so subdued, so encrusted, rendered so mellow by the passing of the generations! As long as Fate allowed him to eke out his days amid old time-weathered concretions, like this KingтАЩs Barton Tower, he could never touch certain abysses of misery! Here in these West Country places he was at any rate spared the atrocity of feeling the pinch of lifeтАЩs dilemmas against a background of monstrous modern inventions. The long, cold clutch of scientific discovery, laid, like metallic fingers, upon the human pulse, could not despoil the dignity of existence here; though the invasion by such inhuman forces had already begun!

тАЬLong may this tower stand, so that men like me can touch its stones, its buttresses, its lichen, its moss, and escape from the dragonтАЩs-tail of the stinging present!тАЭ

He was conscious of a stealthy step behind him, and, turning round, he found Jason Otter at his side.

тАЬYou are enjoying yourself looking at his grave,тАЭ the poet began; тАЬand I donтАЩt blame you. I like looking at the graves of people IтАЩve known. But you go further than I could go, Solent. You are the clever one, the wise one, the old cunning one! You can enjoy looking at a grave though you never knew the person whoтАЩs in it.тАЭ

тАЬYou canтАЩt expect me not to be interested in Redfern, can you?тАЭ retorted Wolf, a little crustily.

тАЬOf course not. ThatтАЩs just it. We all feel an interestтБатАФa nice, merry interestтБатАФin being alive when someone else is dead. He only came down here for money,тАЭ he added unexpectedly, тАЬlike you!тАЭ

тАЬIf I came for it, I assure you I donтАЩt get it,тАЭ said Wolf.

Jason chuckled a great deal at this remark. Then he grew grave. тАЬIтАЩve got a poem here IтАЩd like to read to you, if it wouldnтАЩt spoil your pleasure in looking at this young manтАЩs grave. I wonтАЩt, if it would.тАЭ

тАЬIтАЩve looked all I want to look,тАЭ said Wolf; тАЬso do read me what youтАЩve got there. IтАЩm glad of any excuse not to go round the field and hear so much talk.тАЭ

тАЬSit down, then, a minuteтБатАКтБатАж do you mind?тАЭ

The two men sat down at the base of the wall and leaned their backs against it, facing the school-treat meadow. Jason produced from his pocket a small notebook, which he opened very deliberately upon his knee.

тАЬItтАЩs about white seaweed,тАЭ said Jason Otter.

тАЬI didnтАЩt know it was ever white,тАЭ said Wolf.

тАЬEverything is white at one time or another,тАЭ retorted Jason. тАЬYouтАЩll be white enough yourself, one fine day!тАЭ

тАЬIf it only gets white when itтАЩs dead,тАЭ argued Wolf obstinately, тАЬI donтАЩt think itтАЩs much of a subject. I like the idea of seaweed being white in the way chalk is white or daisies are white; but if it just fades and bleachesтБатАКтБатАж I donтАЩt think much of that.тАЭ

тАЬItтАЩs no good abusing me before youтАЩve heard it,тАЭ said Jason; тАЬbut, of course, we know this business of reading our writings is what your friend Darnley would call impolite.тАЭ

тАЬGo on, man, go on!тАЭ cried Wolf. тАЬIтАЩm listening.тАЭ

And the poet began to read.

тАЬWhite SeaweedтАЭтБатАКтБатАж

He repeated these words a second time, gathering his energy.

тАЬWhite Seaweed.тАЭ

тАЬFor GodтАЩs sake,тАЭ cried Wolf, тАЬget on with it! TheyтАЩll catch sight of us in a moment and then itтАЩll all be spoilt.тАЭ

Jason accepted this impatience with unruffled equanimity, and began in a low voice; but, gathering confidence as he proceeded, he read the poem from beginning to end without a pause.

White as the foam in the track of a whale
As he spouts and sports for a thousand miles
Where the waters slope round the planetтАЩs rim,
Beyond the continents and the isles,
White as the foam that follows him
Where thereтАЩs never a masthead nor a sail.

Drowned and dead from their sunken ships,
Drift the bodies of boys and girls;
White are they as they float and drift,
Their hair like flotsam, their breasts like pearls,
While the grey tides lift them, or cease to lift,
And the green tides gurgle between their lips.

FishesтАЩ eyes in the cold grey deep,
Staring and waiting, waiting and staring,
SeagullsтАЩ beaks on the tops of the wave,
The same eternal quest are sharing;
But the dark, wet, purple, slippery grave
Holds safe those bodies in untouched sleep.

And out of the flesh of those bodies light,
In their dark, wet, purple, slippery bed,
A seaweed grows that is soft as silk,
White as the moon on St.┬аAlbanтАЩs Head,
Moss-like, fern-like, white as milk.тБатАФ
The fingers of Mary are not more white!

Oh! White as the horn of GodтАЩs unicorn,
That seaweed lies upon Redcliff bay,
Lies in the spindrift on Redcliff sands,
Fling all your wicked thoughts away!
Take off your shoes; anoint your hands!
Than to touch such seaweed with careless scorn
тАЩTwere better never to have been born!

JasonтАЩs voice sank; and that peculiar silence ensued which is fuller of electric crosscurrents than anything else in the worldтБатАКтБатАж the silence produced by the falling of the seminal drops of verbal creationтБатАКтБатАж upon an alien mind.

тАЬI like it very much,тАЭ murmured Wolf at last. And he thought to himself, тАЬThe beggar has his own peculiar imagination.тАЭ

Then he said aloud: тАЬItтАЩs one of your best poems, Jason. I donтАЩt think itтАЩs quite up to the тАШSlowworm of Lenty,тАЩ but it does you credit and I congratulate you. What did you exactly mean by that last verse? Did you mean that there are people in the world whose wicked thoughts are aroused by white seaweed, or did you just mean the ordinary stupidity of human beings?тАЭ

тАЬItтАЩs not my business to explain what I mean,тАЭ said Jason. тАЬItтАЩs my business to write. I can see what you think. You think that I just string words together as they come into my head! It isnтАЩt as easy to write a poem as you seem to imagine.тАЭ

тАЬWhy do you write so often about water and about drowned people?тАЭ asked Wolf. тАЬYour pond-elf in тАШThe SlowwormтАЩ gave me a weird feeling; and this seaweed of yours, growing out of drowned bodiesтБатАФтАЭ

тАЬYou neednтАЩt go on!тАЭ interrupted Jason. тАЬOf course, I canтАЩt expect anyone to like my poetry who lives by copying out the liquorish thoughts of a doting old fool. We all want to be glorified. My poetry is all IтАЩve got and I ought never to have read it to you. I ought to have known IтАЩd only get abuse. ItтАЩs this wanting to be glorified thatтАЩs the mistake. A person ought to be satisfied if he can get his meals three times a day, without having to dance attendance on some silly old man or some ugly old woman!тАЭ

Wolf swept this aside. тАЬDo you have in your mind any definite people when you make the newts and tadpoles tease the pond-elf, and when you make these fish and gulls want to eat these youthful bodies?тАЭ

JasonтАЩs face wrinkled with delight at this.

тАЬYouтАЩre afraid I might bring you in!тАЭ he chuckled. тАЬI wouldnтАЩt mind not being glorified if I could make your friend Urquhart agitate himself as much as you do over my poems.тАЭ

Wolf had no time to reply to this; for, to his considerable surprise, he perceived his mother and Gerda, arm in arm, advancing towards their retreat.

Both he and Jason struggled simultaneously to their feet and moved towards the two women. Mrs.┬аSolent began speaking with her accustomed high-pitched ironical intonation.

тАЬDonтАЩt take it off, Mr.┬аOtter,тАЭ she said, when the poet raised his hand to his hat. тАЬI know how you hate the sun; and it is hot today; though the hotter it is the more I enjoy it, though I think our pretty Gerda here agrees with you.тАЭ

Jason, who had succeeded with a certain embarrassment in lifting his straw-hat a few inches from his head in a stiff, perpendicular direction, pulled it down once more over his forehead with grateful relief.

тАЬWhatтАЩs this?тАЭ said Wolf, trying to conceal his discomfort under an airy jauntiness. тАЬWhatтАЩs this between you two?тАЭ

тАЬYour mother and I have had several walks together,тАЭ said Jason, тАЬand she knows my ways.тАЭ

тАЬSo well as to take a great liberty!тАЭ exclaimed the handsome lady, whose brown eyes were shining with radiant exultation. And as she spoke she stepped to JasonтАЩs side and poked something, with her light-gloved fingers, into its place under his hat.

While this was proceeding, the expression upon the poetтАЩs face made Wolf astonished. It was the queerest mixture of physical repulsion with pleasurable, masochistic submission. He was amazed at his motherтАЩs audacity.

тАЬWhat is it that you wear under your hat, Mr.┬аOtter?тАЭ asked Gerda innocently.

The strange man looked at her with a very peculiar expressionтБатАФan expression that baffled Wolf altogether. Then a most beautiful look came into his grey eyes, a look infinitely wistful and sorrowful, the sort of look that a disguised and persecuted god, lost among some savage race that knew him not nor could have comprehended him if it had known him, might have worn; and he replied gently: тАЬI feel the sun, young lady. I find cabbage-leaves a great help. But todayтАЭтБатАФand here he smiled a disarming smileтБатАФтАЬtoday itтАЩs a rhubarb-leaf.тАЭ

Having said this, and with a courtly bend of his body that would have done credit to a royal personage, Jason Otter moved off, making his way, with careful manoeuvring to avoid any encounter with the crowd, towards that part of the field where the old men of the village, seated on wooden benches, were partaking of cakes and cider.

тАЬI hope you havenтАЩt offended him, Mother,тАЭ muttered Wolf.

тАЬI donтАЩt think so,тАЭ cried Gerda. тАЬWhat a nice man he is, Wolf! I like him ever so much better than Darnley.тАЭ

тАЬThatтАЩs because DarnleyтАЩs my best friend,тАЭ said Wolf. тАЬItтАЩs a law of nature, sweetheart, isnтАЩt it, Mother?тАЭ

But Mrs.┬аSolent completely disregarded this little passage between them.

тАЬWhat Gerda and I came for,тАЭ she said, тАЬwas to ask you to show us Mr.┬аRedfernтАЩs grave. GerdaтАЩs never seen it, though her father made the headstone, and IтАЩve never seen it, though IтАЩve asked Mr.┬аUrquhart a hundred times to show it to me.тАЭ

тАЬItтАЩs not hard to find,тАЭ said Wolf drily. тАЬYou could have gone any day by yourself.тАЭ

тАЬWhatтАЩs the sport in that?тАЭ laughed the lady, still displaying the same undercurrent of secret excitement. тАЬThe fun of looking at graves is all in the person you look at them withтБатАКтБатАж isnтАЩt it, Gerda? IтАЩm sure you must have enjoyed yourself watching all the fuss people make!тАЭ

тАЬI canтАЩt help my father being a monument-maker,тАЭ said Gerda gravely. тАЬItтАЩs a trade, like any other trade.тАЭ

тАЬIтАЩm not quarrelling with your fatherтАЩs profession, child,тАЭ Mrs.┬аSolent rapped out. тАЬIтАЩm only saying that thereтАЩs no sport in looking at graves by oneself; and I do want to see this one.тАЭ

тАЬThere it is, then, Mother!тАЭ cried Wolf, almost peevishly. тАЬCanтАЩt you see?тБатАКтБатАж the tall stone one thereтБатАКтБатАж no! over thereтБатАКтБатАж nearer the Tower,тАЭ and he pointed with his stick.

тАЬI want to go up to it,тАЭ said Mrs.┬аSolent obstinately, тАЬand so does Gerda. She told me so just now. WeтАЩre both sick to death of swinging long-legged girls. I donтАЩt want to see any more frills or garters for the rest of my life.тАЭ

тАЬWell, come on, then,тАЭ said Wolf petulantly. тАЬYou can climb over this, canтАЩt you, Mother? I suppose Bob WeevilтАЩs making himself useful at the swings, eh?тАЭ

Whatever demon it was that made him indulge in this jocularity, its result was immediate.

Gerda turned on him fiercely. тАЬDonтАЩt be so vulgar, Wolf. BobтАЩs playing cricket, and soтАЩs Lobbie. You ought to know better than to make remarks like that!тАЭ

тАЬDonтАЩt push me, Wolf.тАЭ It was his mother speaking, as she began scrambling over the low moss-grown wall. тАЬGive me your hand;тБатАКтБатАж no! give me your hand.тАЭ

Soon they were all three standing by RedfernтАЩs grave.

тАЬPoor boy!тАЭ sighed Mrs.┬аSolent. тАЬDo you know, Wolf, I heard Roger Monk talking in a queer way last week. I was asking him about this boyтАЩs death, and he spoke in such a funny tone about it. He almost implied that it was a case of suicide. Have you heard anything of that sort?тАЭ

тАЬOh, just rumours, Mother,тАЭ replied Wolf casually; тАЬjust rumours and village-gossip. IтАЩve never heard of an inquest, or anything like that. I believe he died in his bed.тАЭ

тАЬFather talks queer about it too,тАЭ said Gerda. тАЬBut do look at that! Is that a mole or a rabbit?тАЭ

тАЬI donтАЩt know,тАЭ said Wolf vaguely. It did not interest him in any particular way that this newly-grown-over mound should have been burrowed into or scraped at. After his many years of London life, the ways of moles, rabbits, dogs, foxes, were all equally arbitrary, equally unpredictable. It was, however, brought home to him now that there was something exceptional in this phenomenon; for Gerda, oblivious of the risk of grass-stains upon her summer frock, went down hurriedly on her knees and began fumbling with her bare fingers in the disordered clay, scooping up little handsfuls of dry brown earth with one hand and filtering them thoughtfully into the hollow palm of the other hand.

тАЬAre you looking for Mr.┬аRedfernтАЩs bones?тАЭ enquired Mrs.┬аSolent in her most airy manner. тАЬYou look like that pretty girl in the poem, leaning over her Pot of Basil; doesnтАЩt she, Wolf?тАЭ And touching the mound with the tip of her green parasol she put her head a little to one side and began quoting from the poem in question in a mock-sentimental intonationтБатАКтБатАж

тАЬAnd she forgot the stars, the moon, the sun;
And she forgot the blue above the trees;
And she forgot the dells where waters run;
And she forgot the chilly autumn breeze.
She had no knowledgeтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬDonтАЩt, Mother,тАЭ interrupted Wolf crossly, тАЬGerda knows what sheтАЩs doing.тАЭ

The unequalled lines roused their response in him, as independently of the mocking tone in which they were spoken as beautiful limbs under a ridiculous disguise; but this response only annoyed him the more.

тАЬWhat is it, sweetheart?тАЭ he cried. тАЬIs it a rabbit? I didnтАЩt know rabbits ever burrowed in churchyards.тАЭ

тАЬItтАЩs a mole,тАЭ said Mrs.┬аSolent.

It was WolfтАЩs turn to mutter something nowтБатАКтБатАж

тАЬWell said, old mole! canst work iтАЩ the earth so fast?
A worthy pioneer! Once more remove, good friends.тАЭ

тАЬWhatтАЩs that? You know perfectly well itтАЩs a mole, Gerda,тАЭ said Mrs.┬аSolent. Gerda remained silent. She lifted some of the loose earth to her face and smelt it. Then she leapt to her feet, shook out her skirt, and rubbed the palms of her hands together. тАЬI give it up,тАЭ she said. тАЬIt isnтАЩt a rabbit. ThereтАЩs no smell of a fox either. It may have been a dog.тАЭ

тАЬA moleтБатАКтБатАж a mole!тАЭ repeated the older woman.

тАЬA mole!тАЭ muttered Gerda, with the profound sarcasm of the country-bred; and Wolf caught a little red flush on her cheeks like a crimson shadow on a mother-of-pearl shell. тАЬWell! we canтАЩt do anything, anyway,тАЭ she said. тАЬItтАЩs silly to fuss ourselves. Bother! IтАЩve got some grit in my shoe!тАЭ

тАЬIt spoils the look of the grave completely, this great mole-hole,тАЭ said Mrs.┬аSolent. Then her face lit up, and she opened her parasol with an eager click. тАЬThis is a bit of sport,тАЭ she cried. тАЬLetтАЩs fill the thing up! Never mind about the school-treat. Where does Valley keep his spade? We only want a spade and a roll of turf. I saw some loose turf lying about in our garden. Come on, Wolf! LetтАЩs go over and get it, and ask Valley where he keeps his shovel.тАЭ

Her face was full of animation now, and her eyes shone. Her grey hair and black Gainsborough hat framed the vivid cheeks of youth. The way she tilted her parasol as she spoke had something adventurous, almost hoydenish.

тАЬCome, Wolf, letтАЩs get that turf,тАЭ said Mrs.┬аSolent. тАЬWe must ask Valley where he keeps his spade.тАЭ

As Wolf turned to follow his mother on this impetuous quest, he caught sight of Gerda, struggling with the strap of her shoe, as she propped herself with one hand upon RedfernтАЩs headstone. There was such a look of defiant anger on her face that he halted irresolutely.

тАЬOh, go, if you want to, Wolf!тАЭ she cried. тАЬIтАЩm sure I donтАЩt want to keep you. It isnтАЩt often, though, that I get a chance of enjoying myself, working like I do in that dark kitchen all the time!тАЭ

Mrs.┬аSolent gave her a steady, surprised stare.

тАЬI wonтАЩt keep him long, if you want him for your game,тАЭ she said. тАЬI can fill this hole up by myself, if you just get me the spade and the turf, Wolf.тАЭ

The flush in GerdaтАЩs cheeks grew deeper. тАЬI think itтАЩs a shame! Why did you bring me here at all, Wolf, if we werenтАЩt going to do something nice? I donтАЩt want to spend this afternoon doing what I do every day in the week.тАЭ

Mrs.┬аSolent gave Wolf a quick, surprised look, full of airy pityтБатАФa look that said, тАЬYou poor boy, how awful for you to be at the beck and call of such a child!тАЭ But aloud she remarked:

тАЬItтАЩs all right, Gerda. We wonтАЩt spoil your sport. Run along to your friends. I wonтАЩt keep him long.тАЭ

But GerdaтАЩs suppressed anger had mounted so high by this time that there could be no such easy d├йnouement.

She held up her rounded chin and tossed back her head. Then, clasping her hands behind her, with her heels close together at the edge of the grave, she regarded Mrs.┬аSolent with flashing eyes.

тАЬOf course WolfтАЩs on your side. Of course heтАЩll love to fool about with your spades and turf, when itтАЩs my one real treat of the whole summer! You two are both the same. You only think of yourselves and what you want. If itтАЩs the silliest thing, like this nonsense about a mole, and every sensible person knows what a molehill is, it must come first, before everything, just because youтАЩve thought of it! Oh, yes, I saw you smiling at him just now, when my shoe came off. You couldnтАЩt have looked much different if my stockings had been full of holes! Everyone canтАЩt buy high-class London things; but I tell you our Blacksod shops be as good as they be any day in the week!тАЭ

тАЬWell, Wolf,тАЭ said Mrs.┬аSolent calmly, holding her parasol at a correct garden-party angle and letting her high spirits drop away, тАЬthe best thing you can do is to take your pretty young wife back to her friendsтАЩ games.тАЭ

тАЬMy friendsтАЩ games!тАЭ retorted the indignant girl. тАЬIтАЩm as old as anyone, considering all I put up with!тАЭ

тАЬMy dear child,тАЭ said the elder lady gently. тАЬThereтАЩs really no reason for this excitement. Do try and calm yourself, and letтАЩs all go back quietly. IтАЩm sure IтАЩm quite ready to give up my idea if it spoils your pleasure. DonтАЩt, for mercyтАЩs sake, make such a mountain out of this molehill. I only thought of filling up this hole as a bit of sport, and because school-treats are so boring.тАЭ

Her words were soothing; but there was something in the tilt of her eyebrows, as she glanced at Wolf, which made him realize that she was less unruffled than she appeared. He knew of old that the one thing in the world she hated was any display of temper or anything resembling a тАЬscene.тАЭ His own mind at this moment was unable to resist its furtive commentary upon the way Chance had managed to stage this encounter between the two. He had noticed these tricks before. It was as if there were some special aesthetic laws which Chance delighted to obey; and it always gave him a peculiar satisfaction to contemplate this bizarre rhythm. At such moments he found himself sacrificing action, emotion, sympathy, every human attribute, in a sort of ecstatic pondering over what this artistry of Chance was accomplishing. He felt as if he were in the presence of the unrolling of a psychic map. The figures on this mapтБатАФhis mother with her green parasol, Gerda with her grass-stained dressтБатАФwere a sort of eddying vortex of significance upon a stream that was always rippling itself into mystic diagrams! Chance, in fact, was forever at work fulfilling its own secret aesthetic laws; but every now and then, as at this fatal moment, its creation became especially vivid, and the whole тАЬpsychic mapтАЭ upon that flowing stream grew violently and intensely agitated. The circle of ripples he was now contemplating with this inhuman detachment had two circumferences, namely, the angry consciousness of Gerda and the supercilious consciousness of his mother; but below them bothтБатАФdown there on the quiet river-floorтБатАФwas the discolored, decomposed, unrecognizable face of the young Redfern.

тАЬYouтАЩve never liked my marrying him!тАЭ It was GerdaтАЩs voice he heard now, as he awoke from his metaphysical trance to realize that part of his motherтАЩs last remarks had fallen upon nothing but the surface of his mind.

тАЬIтАЩve always been an outsider to both of you,тАЭ the angry girl went on. тАЬYouтАЩve always despised me and my family, and done your best to make him despise us.тАЭ

тАЬI have the greatest respect for your family, my good child. No one who knows your father can possibly help it. Come now! It really wonтАЩt do for us to make Wolf embarrassed like this. IтАЩve the utmost respect for your people, Gerda, and IтАЩm sure my son couldnтАЩt have married a lovelier creature than you are, even at this moment! But do come, now, both of you, and letтАЩs get back to the field. Mr.┬аUrquhart will be quite lost among those boys without WolfтАЩs help.тАЭ

She laid her hand with a soothing gesture upon the girlтАЩs wrist; but the glance she gave Wolf was full of a mocking resignation that threw a screen round them and railed off this ill-advised proletarian. GerdaтАЩs behaviour on the other side of this barrier became so irrational that it could only excite well-bred surprise! But the girl tossed her hand away.

тАЬMr.┬аUrquhart, indeed!тАЭ she cried. тАЬA nice sort you are out here, you KingтАЩs Barton gentry! Why, IтАЩve never cared even to tell Wolf all IтАЩve heard Dad say about what some folks do in this dirty village.тАЭ Her voice grew louder, as her long-suppressed feelings burst forth. Wolf had fancied in his simplicity that his motherтАЩs airy propitiations had disarmed the girl; but he underrated both GerdaтАЩs perspicacity and her pride.

There was something else on GerdaтАЩs mind, too, beyond her personal indignation. What actually, he wondered, were these Blacksod gossips saying? He looked at the girl with a kind of paralyzed helplessness, and again the thought struck him how neat a stroke of chance it was that RedfernтАЩs grave should be the background of her outburst.

тАЬSome of you gentry,тАЭ she went on fiercely, тАЬdonтАЩt lie abed with decent consciences like my folks! Why, they do say down at FarmerтАЩs Rest that landlord Round do keep his bed, and that Squire Urquhart can get no peace by night or by day, because of what do taunt their minds over this poor young man.тАЭ

In spite of his discomfort, Wolf couldnтАЩt help feeling faintly amused at GerdaтАЩs struggle to keep the insidious Dorset dialect out of her speech, a struggle that grew less and less availing as her agitation rose.

тАЬAnd these be the high-class people that you think so superior to respectable plain folk like my dear Dad!тАЭ Her voice had a quiver in it at this point that made Wolf cry out, тАЬGerda! Gerda darling!тАЭ But she did not break down. On the contrary, her tone grew stronger and more defiant. тАЬLike my dear Dad,тАЭ she went on, тАЬwho never in his whole life said an evil word to anyone.
